crossref.publisher: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
crossref.abstract: <jats:title>Abstract</jats:title><jats:p>Strongly correlated materials respond sensitively to external perturbations such as strain, pressure, and doping. In the recently discovered superconducting infinite-layer nickelates, the superconducting transition temperature can be enhanced via only ~ 1% compressive strain-tuning with the root of such enhancement still being elusive. Using resonant inelastic x-ray scattering (RIXS), we investigate the magnetic excitations in infinite-layer PrNiO<jats:sub>2</jats:sub> thin films grown on two different substrates, namely SrTiO<jats:sub>3</jats:sub> (STO) and (LaAlO<jats:sub>3</jats:sub>)<jats:sub>0.3</jats:sub>(Sr<jats:sub>2</jats:sub>TaAlO<jats:sub>6</jats:sub>)<jats:sub>0.7</jats:sub> (LSAT) enforcing different strain on the nickelates films. The magnon bandwidth of PrNiO<jats:sub>2</jats:sub> shows only marginal response to strain-tuning, in sharp contrast to the enhancement of the superconducting transition temperature <jats:italic>T</jats:italic><jats:sub>c</jats:sub> in the doped superconducting samples. These results suggest the bandwidth of spin excitations of the parent compounds is similar under strain while <jats:italic>T</jats:italic><jats:sub><jats:italic>c</jats:italic></jats:sub> in the doped ones is not, and thus provide important empirics for the understanding of superconductivity in infinite-layer nickelates.</jats:p>
title: Magnetic excitations in strained infinite-layer nickelate PrNiO2 films
